metadata.title: Guideline on quality, non-clinical and clinical aspects of medicinal products containing genetically modified cells
metadata.document_version: Revision 1 (corr) — adopted by CAT 9 October 2020, by CHMP 12 November 2020, effective 1 June 2021
metadata.issuing_body: European Medicines Agency / Committee for Advanced Therapies (CAT)
metadata.scope_note: Covers all genetically modified cells for human use (autologous, allogeneic, primary, established cell lines) modified by viral/non-viral vectors, mRNA, or genome editing tools. Excludes microbial origin cells.

rules.rule_text: For ex vivo gene transfer, GMP shall apply from the bank system used to produce the vector onwards. For genome editing approaches, GMP shall apply from the bank system used to produce the modifying tools (vectors, mRNA, proteins, components) onwards.
rules.verbatim_quote: For ex vivo gene transfer, the tools used to genetically modify the cells shall be, as appropriate, the vector (e.g. viral or non-viral vector) and the components to produce them. Principles of good manufacturing practice (GMP) shall apply from the bank system used to produce the vector onwards. For genome editing approaches, the tools used to genetically modify the cells shall be, as appropriate, the vector (viral or non-viral vector) carrying the nucleic acid sequences encoding the modifying enzyme... the principles of GMP shall apply from the bank system used to produce these materials onwards.

rules.rule_text: The use of unrelated DNA sequences, such as selection markers, that can end up in the final genetically modified cells must be avoided unless justified.
rules.verbatim_quote: The use of unrelated DNA sequences, such as selection markers, that can end up in the final genetically modified cells should be avoided unless justified.
rules.exceptions: Justification required if avoidance is not possible.

rules.rule_text: Prior to use, the transfer vector must be sterile and free from any unwanted viral contamination, including helper or hybrid viruses, adventitious contamination, and replication-competent vectors for vectors intended to be replication deficient. A validated, sensitive assay (or combination of assays) must be used for RCV testing. Use of non-purified transfer vectors in the transduction process must be avoided.
rules.verbatim_quote: Prior to its use, the transfer vector is expected to be sterile and should be shown to be free from any unwanted viral contamination, including helper or hybrid viruses such as in AAV production systems, adventitious contamination or replication-competent vectors for vectors intended to be replication deficient. For the latter, a validated, sensitive assay (or combination of assays), such as a quantitative PCR assay complemented with an infectivity assay in permissive cells, should be used. Use of non-purified transfer vectors in the transduction process should be avoided.

rules.rule_text: Starting materials used for production of genetically modified cells and genome edited products must be carefully qualified to assure a consistent manufacturing process. Detailed information must be provided on manufacturing process, control, characterisation, process development, validation, analytical procedures, and stability. Starting material must be stored under controlled and optimal conditions to maintain critical characteristics within the parameters of the clinically tested batches.
rules.verbatim_quote: Starting materials used for the production of genetically modified cells and genome edited products shall be carefully qualified to assure a consistent manufacturing process... The starting material should be stored under controlled and optimal conditions to ensure maintenance of critical characteristics for the intended use and, in particular, to ensure an acceptable level of consistency in product quality, that should be maintained within the parameters of the clinically tested batches.

rules.rule_text: Viral safety and TSE risk minimisation measures must be adopted for any reagent or material of animal origin used in cell culture, transduction/transfection, or subsequent manufacturing steps, in accordance with Ph. Eur. General Chapter 5.2.12.
rules.source_section: Section 4.1.2 – Other materials, reagents and excipients
rules.verbatim_quote: Viral safety as well as measures taken to minimise the risk of transmitting agents causing Transmissible Spongiform Encephalopathies (TSE) of any reagent or material of animal origin should be adopted. Recombinant proteins such as enzymes, antibodies, cytokines, growth or adhesion factors should be characterised and controlled, where appropriate and relevant, in accordance with the principles described in Ph. Eur. 5.2.12.

rules.rule_text: RCV testing as an in-process test is not required provided that: (a) absence of RCV has been demonstrated at the viral vector starting material level using a validated and sensitive assay, AND (b) RCV formation during manufacturing of the genetically modified cells can be excluded. A risk assessment must be presented to address the potential generation of RCVs during manufacturing.
rules.verbatim_quote: Replication competent virus (RCV) testing as an in-process test is not deemed necessary, provided that absence of RCV has been demonstrated (using validated and sensitive assay(s)) at the level of the viral vector starting material and that RCV formation during manufacturing of the genetically modified cells can be excluded. In this case, a risk assessment should be presented to address the potential generation of RCVs during manufacturing.
rules.exceptions: Both conditions must be met for RCV in-process testing to be waived.

rules.rule_text: Retention samples of genetically modified cell batches must be stored for future analysis whenever feasible.
rules.verbatim_quote: Whenever feasible, retention samples should be stored for future analysis.
rules.exceptions: Feasibility is acknowledged as a limitation.

rules.rule_text: Genetic modification must be carried out using a validated manufacturing process. When using integrating vectors (e.g. lentivirus, retrovirus), multiplicity of infection (MOI) must be kept at the minimum shown to be effective by transduction efficiency studies and clinical studies. For genome editing protocols, generation of on- and off-target modifications must be addressed as part of process development and characterisation, and a risk assessment presented.
rules.verbatim_quote: Genetic modification should be carried out using a validated manufacturing process. When using integrating vectors (e.g. LV and RV), multiplicity of infection should be kept at the minimum shown to be effective by transduction efficiency studies and clinical studies. For genome editing protocols, generation of on- and off-target modifications should be addressed as part of process development and characterisation. A risk assessment should be presented to address the potential appearance of off-target modifications during manufacturing.

rules.rule_text: Process validation for genetically modified cell products must address, as applicable: absence of adventitious contaminants, absence of modifying enzymes and nucleic acids, removal of infectious particles, remaining free vector, transduction/transfection efficiency, vector copy number, transgene identity and integrity, level of transgene expression, structure and function of expressed molecule(s), removal of target nucleic acid sequences when appropriate, and reduction of impurities associated with the genetic modification.
rules.verbatim_quote: In addition to the requirements described for process validation in the Guideline on human cell-based medicinal products (EMEA/CHMP/410869/2006), the following aspects should be addressed, as applicable: absence of adventitious contaminants, absence of modifying enzymes and nucleic acids, removal of infectious particles, remaining free vector, transduction/transfection efficiency, vector copy number, transgene identity and integrity (and of other regions as needed), level of transgene expression, structure and function of the expressed molecule(s), removal or elimination of the target nucleic acid sequences when appropriate, removal or reduction of impurities associated with the genetic modification.
rules.exceptions: Limited cell/tissue availability acknowledged; reduced validation may be offset by additional in-process testing.

rules.rule_text: All changes in the manufacturing process of either the product or starting materials require assessment of risk and appropriate comparability studies according to ICH Q5E principles, comparing pre- and post-change product to assess the impact of any observed difference on quality attributes as it relates to safety and efficacy.
rules.verbatim_quote: It is important that all changes introduced during development are clearly identified within the dossier. In addition, appropriate comparability studies are needed in order to: i) compare pre- and post-change product and ii) assess the impact of any observed difference on the quality attributes as it relates to safety and efficacy of the product... Appropriate comparability studies according to the principles outlined in ICH Topic Q5E for biotechnological/biological products should be conducted to demonstrate comparability of the pre- and post-change product.

rules.rule_text: For high-risk changes in manufacturing process of recombinant starting materials (e.g. manufacturing site change), comparability must include: full vector sequencing, presence of capsid proteins, absence of replication-competent virus, determination of process and product-related impurities, stability, and impact on relevant CQAs of the final product including transduction/transfection efficiency, vector copy number, levels of transgene expression, and on- and off-target modifications.
rules.source_section: Section 4.2.6.1 – Changes in the manufacturing process of the recombinant starting materials
rules.verbatim_quote: In case they are not part of the release specification, comparability for high-risk changes should include, as appropriate: full vector sequencing, presence of capsid proteins, absence of replication-competent virus, determination of process and product-related impurities as well as stability. In addition to the comparability study of the recombinant starting material, studies to demonstrate the impact on relevant CQAs of the final product should be undertaken. These include testing transduction/transfection efficiency, vector copy number (VCN), levels of transgene expression, on- and off-target modifications, etc.
rules.exceptions: For similar defined manufacturing steps, previously conducted validation may be leveraged for closely related products.

rules.rule_text: Rigorous characterisation of the genetically modified cell medicinal product is essential and must identify Critical Quality Attributes (CQAs). Characterisation must address as appropriate: cell identity and viability, cell phenotype/morphology, heterogeneity, proliferation/differentiation capacity, cell functionality, transduction/transfection efficiency, sequence and integrity of transgene, genetic stability, identity and activity of expressed gene product, vector copy number, vector integration profile, vector release from cells, vector replication competence, persistence of genome editing tools, and on-target and off-target genetic modifications.
rules.verbatim_quote: Rigorous characterisation of the genetically modified cell medicinal product (either alone or in combination with a medical device) is essential... The use of a range of appropriately qualified molecular, biological and immunological methods for the following characteristics should be addressed, as appropriate: cell identity and viability, cell phenotype / morphology, heterogeneity of the cell population... transduction/transfection efficiency (e.g. percentage of transduced cells), sequence and integrity of transgene, genetic stability upon in vitro proliferation and/or differentiation, identity and activity of the expressed gene product, vector copy number per transduced/transfected cell, vector integration profile (when applicable)... on-target and off-target genetic modifications.

rules.rule_text: For cells modified using genome editing tools, off-target changes must be identified using at least one sensitive and well-characterised experimental assay in the relevant therapeutic cell type or surrogate settings. Appropriate bioinformatics tools for in silico screening are required. Candidate genomic sites identified in silico must be interrogated by deep-sequencing (or other appropriate technique) in the actual therapeutic cell type. The possibility of large deletions, chromosomal translocations and other large-scale genomic alterations must also be accounted for.
rules.verbatim_quote: For cells modified using genome-editing tools, induced off-target changes should be identified using at least one sensitive and well-characterized experimental assay in the cell type to be used therapeutically or in surrogate settings (e.g. healthy donor cells). In addition, appropriate bioinformatics tools for in silico screening are expected... This set of candidate genomic sites should then be interrogated by deep-sequencing (or other appropriate technique) in the actual cell type to be used therapeutically... The possible occurrence of large deletions, chromosomal translocations and other large-scale genomic alterations should also be accounted for based on the actual profile of on- and off-target edits verified in the treated cells.

rules.rule_text: Identity testing must include assays to detect both the presence of the specific cell population AND the intended genetic modification at DNA level or an assay to detect the presence of the intended product translated from the genetic modification at the protein level.
rules.verbatim_quote: Identity testing should include assays to detect the presence of the specific cell population as well as the intended genetic modification (at DNA level or an assay to detect the presence of the intended product translated from the genetic modification at the protein level). The test methods should be specific for those components.

rules.rule_text: When a viral vector is used for transduction, the level of infectious particles in the final product must be determined and kept below a justified limit. In case of replication deficient viral vectors, tests to show absence of RCV are essential; if RCV absence is demonstrated at other levels, additional testing may be waived by appropriate risk assessment. The assay for RCV must have an appropriate limit of detection, justified in the risk assessment taking into consideration the worst case expressed for a human dose.
rules.verbatim_quote: When a viral vector is used for transduction, the level of infectious particles in the final product should be determined and kept below a justified limit... In the case of replication deficient viral vectors, tests to show the absence of replication-competent viruses (RCV) are essential; however, if absence of RCV is demonstrated at other levels (for instance at the viral vector starting material) no additional testing is required provided that generation of RCVs during manufacturing is ruled out by an appropriate risk assessment. The assay for RCV should have an appropriate limit of detection, justified in the risk assessment taking into consideration the worst case and expressed for a human dose.
rules.exceptions: RCV testing at final product may be waived if demonstrated at vector starting material level with appropriate risk assessment.

rules.rule_text: In case of genome editing, persistence of genome editing tools in the cells must be evaluated. Ideally genome editing tools should no longer be present when the cells are released for clinical use. Where relevant, a release test for the presence of genome editing tools must be included.
rules.verbatim_quote: In case of genome editing, the persistence of genome editing tools in the cells should be evaluated. Ideally genome editing tools should no longer be present when the cells are released for clinical use. The persistence may depend on the vector used to introduce the genome editing tools into the cells. Where relevant a release test for the presence of genome editing tools should be included.

rules.rule_text: The potency test(s) must provide quantitative information on the intended function of the cell and the transgene product. Potency testing must not be limited to cell functionality but must also include other relevant tests such as cell viability, and where relevant, potential to proliferate, differentiate and persist after administration. A reference batch of cells with assigned potency must be established. For genome editing tools used for genetic modification, a reference batch must be established.
rules.verbatim_quote: The potency test(s) should provide, as far as possible, quantitative information on the intended function of the cell and the transgene product... The potency testing should not be limited to cell functionality, but also include other relevant tests, e.g. cell viability. Furthermore, where relevant, release tests for the potential to proliferate, differentiate and persist after administration should be in place. Wherever possible, a reference batch of cells with assigned potency should be established and used to calibrate tests. For tools (e.g. vectors, recombinant proteins) used for genetic modification of cells a reference batch should be established.
rules.exceptions: Biological potency tests in animal tissues should only be considered where a suitable in vitro method cannot be developed (3R principles).

rules.rule_text: Release criteria for genetically modified cell products must include, in addition to general pharmaceutical tests (sterility, endotoxin, appearance): quantity, identity, purity, impurities (product- and process-related), and potency. The copy number of integrated vectors per transduced or transfected cell as read-out for safety and potency must be tested on each batch of final product.
rules.verbatim_quote: In addition to general pharmaceutical tests (e.g. sterility, endotoxin, appearance etc.), release testing should include analysis of quantity, identity, purity, impurities (product- and process-related) and potency... The copy number of integrated vectors per transduced or transfected cell as read-out for safety and potency should be tested on each batch of final product.
rules.exceptions: In exceptional and well justified cases, a two-step release testing program may be used if product material is too limited.

rules.rule_text: For cells transduced with a replication defective vector, the absence of RCV must be demonstrated before clinical use. Omission of RCV analysis at final product level could be justified only if absence of RCV is confirmed at vector release using a validated, sensitive assay (or combination of assays).
rules.verbatim_quote: For cells transduced with a replication defective vector, the absence of RCV should be demonstrated before clinical use. Depending on the risk of RCV formation, omission of analysis for RCV at final product level could be justified in case absence of RCV is confirmed at vector release using a validated, sensitive assay (or combination of assays).
rules.exceptions: Omission justified only if confirmed at vector release level with validated sensitive assay.

rules.rule_text: Stability studies, including in-use stability studies, must be conducted per the Guideline on human cell-based medicinal products. Quality attributes followed during stability studies must be stability-indicating (and quantitative) and be able to detect clinically meaningful changes in the product.
rules.verbatim_quote: Stability studies, including in-use stability studies, should be conducted according to the principles described in the Guideline on human cell-based medicinal products (EMEA/CHMP/410869/2006). Quality attributes to be followed during stability studies should be defined on the basis of characterisation studies. They should be stability indicating (and quantitative) and be able to detect clinically meaningful changes in the product.

rules.rule_text: Non-clinical studies for genetically modified cell products must ideally be carried out with batches produced and quality controlled according to the production process in place for clinical studies. If homologous products are used, key parameters for efficacy and safety must be evaluated and compared with clinically produced cells, with differences in production processes and key parameters clearly indicated.
rules.verbatim_quote: Ideally, the non-clinical studies should be carried out with batches of genetically modified cells produced and quality controlled according to the production process in place for clinical studies. If this is not possible, such as when homologous products are used, the key parameters for efficacy and safety of the genetically modified cells used should be evaluated and compared with cells produced and controlled according to the clinical production process. Differences in the production processes as well as differences in the key parameters of the genetically modified cells should be indicated and the potential impact on the predictability of the data should be discussed.
rules.exceptions: Homologous products may be acceptable with comparison data.

rules.rule_text: Proof-of-concept studies must be provided that either support the potential clinical effect and/or prove the anticipated mode of action. The expected effect of the genetic modification must be confirmed at the cellular level. The duration of transgene expression must be evaluated in vivo, unless otherwise justified; unexpected loss or increase of expression must trigger additional investigations.
rules.verbatim_quote: Irrespective of the type of the genetic modification... its expected effect(s) should be confirmed at the cellular level... Proof-of-concept studies that either support the potential clinical effect and/or prove the anticipated mode of action should be provided... The duration of transgene expression should be evaluated in vivo, unless otherwise justified. Any unexpected loss or increase of expression of the transgene should trigger additional investigations in order to determine the reasons for the lost or increased expression.
rules.exceptions: In vitro proof-of-concept may be appropriate where in vivo demonstration is not feasible.

rules.rule_text: Pharmacokinetics studies must be designed to address the in vivo fate (biodistribution, homing, engraftment, stability and persistence) of the genetically modified cells. For secreted gene products, the local and/or systemic exposure and persistence of the transgene product must be included in the analysis.
rules.verbatim_quote: Pharmacokinetics studies should be designed in order to address the in vivo fate (biodistribution, homing, engraftment, stability and persistence) of the genetically modified cells as appropriate... For secreted gene products the local and/or systemic exposure and persistence of the transgene product should be included in the analysis.
rules.exceptions: Omission of germline transmission studies is usually justifiable unless cells carry significantly higher risk for inadvertent germ line transmission.

rules.rule_text: Toxicology studies for genetically modified cell products must specifically address: toxicity related to expression of a transgene, risk of insertional mutagenesis, vector mobilisation and recombination, and product class-specific aspects (CAR and TCR modified T-cells, NK cells, iPS cells, ex vivo gene edited cells).
rules.verbatim_quote: Toxicological endpoints could be addressed in in vitro and/or in vivo studies which should be designed to investigate any adverse effects induced by the genetically modified cells... In addition, the following considerations should be addressed for genetically modified cells: toxicity related to the expression of a transgene, risk of insertional mutagenesis, vector mobilisation and recombination, aspects related to specific product classes such as immune cells (CAR and TCR modified T-cells, NK cells), induced pluripotent stem cells (iPS cells), and ex vivo gene edited cells.
rules.exceptions: GLP principles in relation to ATMPs apply to toxicological studies.

rules.rule_text: When cells are transduced with integrating vectors (gamma-retroviral or lentiviral), the risk of insertional oncogenesis must be carefully evaluated per the Reflection paper on management of clinical risks deriving from insertional mutagenesis (EMA/CAT/190186/2012). Any strategy to reduce the risk, such as the use of self-inactivating (SIN) configuration vectors, must be indicated. For a clonal cell line, the integration site must be determined and vector integration at critical sites (near proto-oncogenes) must be avoided and demonstrated not to induce insertional oncogenesis.
rules.verbatim_quote: When cells are transduced with integrating vectors (e.g. gamma-retroviral or lentiviral), the risk of insertional oncogenesis needs to be carefully evaluated in accordance with the Reflection paper on management of clinical risks deriving from insertional mutagenesis (EMA/CAT/190186/2012)... any strategy aiming at reducing the risk of insertional oncogenesis, for example the use of a gamma-retroviral or lentiviral vector with self-inactivating (SIN) configuration, should be indicated. For a genetically modified clonal cell line, the site(s) of vector integration should be determined and any vector integration at critical sites (e.g. near proto-oncogenes) should be avoided.
rules.exceptions: For allogenic products, in vitro insertion site analysis before administration to humans may be possible.

rules.rule_text: For CAR and TCR modified immune cells, potential on-target/off-tumour and off-target toxicities must be addressed as far as possible either in an appropriate animal model or by an alternative approach using a combination of in silico and in vitro analyses.
rules.verbatim_quote: In case of CAR and TCR modified immune cells potential on-target/off-tumour and off-target toxicities need to be addressed as far as possible either in an appropriate animal model or by an alternative approach using a combination of in silico and in vitro analyses.
rules.exceptions: Alternative in silico/in vitro approach acceptable for CARs containing scFv that only recognizes human epitopes.

rules.rule_text: For TCR modified T-cells, potential mispairing between the introduced TCR chains and the endogenous TCRs must be addressed. Strategies implemented in the design of the introduced TCR chains to reduce potential mispairing must be described and justified.
rules.verbatim_quote: For TCR modified T-cells, potential mispairing between the introduced TCR chains and the endogenous TCRs need to be addressed. Strategies implemented in the design of the introduced TCR chains to reduce potential mispairing needs to be described and justified.

rules.rule_text: For iPS cells: the risk of insertional mutagenicity and oncogenicity associated with integrating viral vectors and induced pluripotency must be addressed. iPS cells carry an inherent risk of tumourigenicity (teratoma formation in vivo). Non-clinical qualification of the level of undifferentiated iPS cell impurities must be addressed in an in vivo study. The functionality of any suicide mechanism included in iPS cells must be confirmed in vivo.
rules.verbatim_quote: iPS cells carry an inherent risk of tumourigenicity as they form teratomas in vivo. Reference is made to the Reflection paper on stem cell-based medicinal products (EMA/CAT/571134/2009)... The non-clinical qualification of the level of undifferentiated iPS cell impurities can be addressed in an in vivo study by e.g. spiking the administered cell product with undifferentiated iPS cells in different quantities... The functionality of such a suicide mechanism should be confirmed in vivo.

rules.rule_text: For genome edited cells, the specificity of the modifying enzyme activity or guide RNA for the targeted genomic sequence must be confirmed in vitro by evaluating on-target and off-target editing in relevant cells. The chosen strategy for addressing off-target activity must include an unbiased genome-wide evaluation of off-target activity in vitro. Effects of genome editing on cell phenotype and physiological functions must be analysed where indicated.
rules.verbatim_quote: In addition to the common requirements for genetically modified cells the following aspects need to be addressed for genome edited cells: the specificity of the modifying enzyme activity or guide RNA for the targeted genomic sequence needs to be confirmed in vitro by evaluating on-target and off-target editing in relevant cells... the chosen strategy for addressing off-target activity should also include an unbiased genome-wide evaluation of off-target activity in vitro. Thereby, the chosen strategy should be justified and the sensitivity of the methods used should be indicated... Effects of genome editing on cell phenotype and physiological functions should be analysed where indicated.
rules.exceptions: In silico analysis may supplement but not replace in vitro evaluation.

rules.rule_text: The clinical trials for genetically modified cell products must be designed to allow benefit/risk assessment based on the specific characteristics of the product (transduced cells), the target population, and existing treatments. The entire therapeutic procedure (collection, conditioning, administration, concomitant medication including immunosuppression) must be investigated when considering the benefit/risk balance.
rules.verbatim_quote: The clinical trials should be designed to allow a benefit/risk assessment, based on the specific characteristics of the product (transduced cells), the target population (case-by-case) and existing treatments... The delivery of the genetically modified cells to the target organ and tissue will require intravascular delivery, percutaneous administration or administration through specific surgical procedures to obtain the intended therapeutic effect. The therapeutic procedure as a whole including the collection procedure (e.g. apheresis, bone-marrow aspiration), myeloablative and/or lymphodepleting regimen, method of administration and eventually the required concomitant medication such as immunosuppressive regimens needs to be investigated when considering the benefit/risk balance.

rules.rule_text: Dose for genetically modified cell products is defined by the number of genetically modified cells per kg, m² body surface or as a flat dose. The starting dose must be safe and have a pharmacological effect; followed by further dose exploration including assessment of maximum tolerable dose in oncology/haematology. The relationship between vector copy number and proportion of transduced cells with engraftment level, in vivo VCN, transgene expression, and clinical data must be explored to determine safe and effective range.
rules.verbatim_quote: The dose is defined by the number of genetically modified cells per kg, m² body surface or as a flat dose, as appropriate. The goal of selecting a starting dose is to identify a dose that is expected to have a pharmacological effect and is safe to use. The assessment of a safe and minimal effective dose should be followed by further dose exploration. If appropriate, a maximum tolerable dose should be assessed... The relationship between vector copy number (VCN) and proportion of transduced cells with engraftment level, in vivo VCN, transgene expression and clinical data should then be explored to determine their safe and effective range.

rules.rule_text: Immunogenicity testing must be conducted throughout the development of genetically modified cell products. Assessment must take into account clinically relevant immune responses to the transgene product and/or to the transduced cells, considering origin of cells (allogeneic vs autologous), disease nature, conditioning regimen, pre-existing immune response, and location of the transgene product.
rules.verbatim_quote: An immune response to the cells and/or the transgene product may compromise efficacy and have an impact on safety, also in cases of single administration. Thus, the immunogenicity testing should be conducted throughout the development. Assessment of immunogenicity needs to take into account clinically relevant immune responses to the transgene product and/or to the transduced cells. The risk for immunogenicity is influenced by the origin of transduced cells (allogeneic versus autologous), the nature of the disease (immune deficient versus immune competent patient population, total absence vs. defective gene product), the type of conditioning regimen, the pre-existing immune response against the transgene product as well as the location of the transgene product (intracellular versus extracellular/secreted).

rules.rule_text: In pivotal trials of autologous genetically modified cell products, all patients enrolled with the intention to initiate treatment must be included in the primary efficacy analysis (ITT principle), including those randomized in a controlled trial or who signed informed consent in a single-arm trial.
rules.verbatim_quote: In trials of autologous genetically modified cell products that are pivotal for benefit risk assessment, all patients all patients that have been enrolled with the intention to initiate treatment, e.g. who have been randomized in a randomized controlled trial or who have signed informed consent in a single-arm trial should be included in the primary efficacy analysis.
rules.exceptions: Supportive analyses can be defined for apheresed population, lymphodepleted population, or treated/infused population if well justified.

rules.rule_text: The possibility that transduced cells, intentionally or not, release any vector or plasmid in vivo must be investigated. Design and extent of such investigations shall depend on properties of the construct and outcome of non-clinical studies. Patients must be monitored for signs of infection from unknown adventitious agents.
rules.verbatim_quote: The possibility that transduced cells, intentionally designed for this purpose or not, release any vector or plasmid in vivo should be investigated. The design and extent of such investigations shall depend on the properties of the construct and the outcome of the non-clinical studies. As for any other biological product, there is a risk of infection from unknown adventitious agents; therefore patients should be monitored for signs of infections.

rules.rule_text: Clinical follow-up of patients treated with genetically modified cells must be ensured per the Guideline on follow-up of patients administered with gene therapy medicinal products (EMEA/CHMP/GTWP/60436/2007). According to current knowledge, a 15-year follow-up is recommended. If there is a risk of late onset adverse events (e.g. leukaemia, secondary malignancies, tumourigenicity), measures must be put in place to address this risk.
rules.verbatim_quote: The clinical follow-up of patients enrolled in clinical trials with genetically modified cells should be ensured according to the principles laid down in the Guideline on follow-up of patients administered with gene therapy medicinal products (EMEA/CHMP/GTWP/60436/2007)... According to the current knowledge, a 15 year follow-up is recommended. If there is a risk of late onset of an adverse event (such as development of leukaemia or other secondary malignancies, or an identified risk of tumourigenicity on a mechanistic basis), measures have to be put in place to address this risk.

rules.rule_text: An EU Risk Management Plan (RMP) is required for genetically modified cell products, per the Guideline on safety and efficacy follow-up and risk management of Advanced Therapy Medicinal Products (EMEA/149995/2008). The RMP must address long-term safety issues including infections, immunogenicity/immunosuppression, malignant transformation, vector dissemination/reactivation, and durability of any associated medical device/biomaterial component. Specific pharmaco-epidemiological studies may be needed.
rules.verbatim_quote: For genetically modified cells, the EU Risk Management Plan (RMP) requirements are described in the Guideline on safety and efficacy follow-up and risk management of Advanced Therapy Medicinal Products (EMEA/149995/2008). Genetically modified cells may need specific long-term studies to monitor safety issues including lack of efficacy and risk of vector dissemination or reactivation. The long-term safety issues, such as infections, immunogenicity/immunosuppression and malignant transformation as well as the durability of the associated medical device/biomaterial component should be addressed in the Risk Management Plan.

rules.rule_text: For CAR-T cells in haemato-oncology, dose-finding studies must be conducted to explore safety, toxicity, and anti-tumour activity at different dose levels, to define the threshold dose required for anti-tumour effect, and to define the recommended dose or dose range for Phase 2 studies. A sound rationale for the dosing regimen in confirmatory studies must be provided based on non-clinical data, clinical data, product-specific factors (transduction efficiency, proliferation capacity), and disease-specific criteria (tumour type, antigen expression, tumour load).
rules.source_section: Annex I – Special clinical considerations on CAR-T-cells in haemato-oncology (Dose finding)
rules.verbatim_quote: dose-finding studies should be conducted to explore safety, toxicity, anti-tumour activity at different dose-levels, to define the threshold dose required for anti-tumour effect and to define the recommended dose or dose range for phase 2 studies. Altogether a sound rationale for the dosing regimen to be used in confirmatory studies should be provided, considering i) non-clinical data and available clinical data, ii) product specific factors like transduction efficiency, proliferation capacity, and iii) disease-specific criteria like tumour type, antigen expression and tumour load.
rules.exceptions: Dose finding from related products may be relevant but has limited extrapolation value due to product-specific factors.

rules.rule_text: For CAR-T cells in haemato-oncology, a comprehensive safety database must be planned that allows full characterisation of CAR-T cell product- and procedure-related adverse events (including apheresis and lymphodepletion) to support a thorough benefit-risk assessment for marketing authorisation. Cytokine release syndrome, CAR T cell related encephalopathy syndrome (CRES), and B cell depletion are known key safety issues requiring specific monitoring and causality assessment.
rules.source_section: Annex I – Special clinical considerations on CAR-T-cells in haemato-oncology (Safety)
rules.verbatim_quote: CAR-T cells are known to elicit acute toxicities that are linked to their pharmacokinetic and pharmacodynamic properties, resulting in a narrow therapeutic index. The main adverse drug reactions (ADRs) described so far are based on the experience with CD19 targeting CAR-T cells in leukemia and lymphoma patients and are described as cytokine release syndrome, CAR T cell related encephalopathy syndrome (CRES) and B cell depletion... Altogether, it is important to plan for a solid and comprehensive data base that allows to fully characterize CAR-T cell product- as well as procedure-related adverse events, including apheresis and lymphodepletion, and to support a thorough benefit-risk assessment for marketing authorisation.
